name: Silicon; single crystalline, Czochralski process; at plant
description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 kg of single crystalline silicon.
This unit process includes raw material acquisition; production; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste; ancillary materials manufacturing; manufacture, and installation of capital equipment.
category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3279: Other Nonmetallic Mineral Product Manufacturing

processDocumentation.timeDescription: The period spans from the publication date of the latest study cited by Frischknecht et al. (2020) and the date of the publication of their report.
processDocumentation.geographyDescription: Frischknecht et al. (2020) is mostly based on Frischknecht et al. (2015). Although the authors of these reports consider the data to be representative of production in the US, data comes from several locations, some undisclosed, some in Germany (see de Wild-Scholten, 2014, for an example)
processDocumentation.technologyDescription: According to Hagedorn and & Hellriegler (1992), cited in Jungbluth et al. (2012) “… silicon parts are broken down to a size of 0.1 to 7.5 cm. In an acid bath with nitric acid, hydrogen fluoride and acetic acid the surface is purified and SiO2 is removed […] The cleaned silicon parts are melted in a crucible and a seed crystal is first dipped into the melt. Then the seed is slowly withdrawn vertically to the melt surface whereby the liquid crystallizes at the seed. The pulling is done under argon inert gas stream.”
processDocumentation.modelingConstantsDescription:
Frischknecht et al (2020) assumed a truck transport of 1000 km for the silicon, and 100 km for everything else. They also estimated a 100-year lifetime and a 1 Mt/year production for the factory. De Wild-Scholten (2014) assumed a standard distance for rail transport of 600 km.
Emissions of water to air are assumed to be 5% of cooling water and 10% of process water. 
Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD), and Total Organic Carbon (TOC), are three ways of measuring effectively the same pollutant. To prevent triple counting, COD and TOC are set to zero, but the actual amounts are given in the description.
processDocumentation.completenessDescription:
Frischknecht et al. (2015, 2020), and de Wild-Scholten (2014) offer no information on cutoff criteria or flows that might have not been considered.
Treatment of Missing Technosphere Data 
Frischknecht et al. (2020) indicates that the material losses (1.5-2 %) are included in the wafer production model. No other information regarding cutoff criteria or flows that might have not been considered is included said report or in Frischknecht et al. (2015).
Mass Balance
The mass balance for this process was not calculated. See also “Treatment of Missing Technosphere Data” above.
processDocumentation.dataSelectionDescription: This unit process was modeled based on Frischknecht et al. (2020) inventory for single crystalline silicon, available on Table 9 of said report. It is based on multiple sources, including a previous iteration of the same inventory (Frischknecht et al., 2015), which in turn was based on de Wild-Scholten (2014). Other sources include Hagedorn (1992) and VDMA (2020).
processDocumentation.dataTreatmentDescription:
The values in the LCI in Frischknecht et al. (2020) reflect the expert judgement of the authors and of the sources they cite, since none suggest a weighted average from different sources/reports.
Process data quality
Based on the information included in the “Data completeness section”, the data quality characteristic (DQC) "Process completeness" was given a score of 1 (>80 % of determined flow have been evaluated and given a value). It is likely the original report went through a review process, but because it is not explicitly mentioned, the data quality characteristic "Process review" was left as default (No documented review) and thus with a score of 5.
processDocumentation.samplingDescription:
System boundary conditions
The system boundary is cradle to gate and includes direct emissions, material and energy use, waste treatment, transport, and infrastructure. 
Data collection
The data that made Frischknecht et al. (2020) LCI is based on previous reports. See “data selection” above and the original source for details.
Uncertainty
Frischknecht et al. (2020) included a standard deviation for all flows, which is repeated here.
processDocumentation.dataCollectionDescription: Data to build this inventory ranges at least from the calendar year 1992 (use of hydrated lime, based on Hagendorn, 1992) to 2019 (electricity use, based on VDMA, 2020)

sources.name: Hagedorn and Hellriegel (1992) Env. flows solar cells

reviews.details: It is likely the original report went through a review process, but because it is not explicitly mentioned, the data quality characteristic "Process review" was left as default (No documented review) and thus with a score of 5.

processDocumentation.intendedApplication:
Situation C2 - Accounting, without system-external interactions - The intended application is a purely descriptive accounting / documentation of the analyzed system excluding interactions with other systems in the LCI model.
The target audience of this model are LCA practitioners, the silicon, solar and related industries and the general public. The inventory is considered largely complete, except for land transformation activities. For that reason, it is not recommended to use this dataset when evaluating land use impact categories. The results of the National Institute of Standards and Technology’s data gap analysis were analyzed using the ISO 21930-LCIA-US impact assessment methodology, as well as Cumulative Energy Demand and a subset of ReCiPe 2016 impact categories.
The original application of this dataset was the identification of data gaps in the Federal LCA Commons (FLCAC) (see "Project" above). However, the intended application is to provide a cradle-to-gate inventory (A1-A3, according to ISO 21930) using exclusively processes currently available in USLCI or generated as part of the project mentioned above, and elementary flows from the Federal Elementary Flow List (FEDEFL) to enable the use of a fuller range of LCIA impact categories than those reported in EPDs. For data quality purposes, the intended application is to provide a US average inventory for the year of publication of the original source.
processDocumentation.projectDescription: This dataset was developed as part of the “Public Life Cycle Inventory Data Gap Analysis for Silicon-based Photovoltaic panels” National Institute of Standards and Technology’s Technical Note.

dataSetOwner.name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
